ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Burmese dried venison salad. FOODON_03315595 (meat salad food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers venison as a raw ingredient. No composite code exists for dried venison salad as a prepared dish. Used as closest parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese dried venison salad or sufficiently close prepared meat salad concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese dried venison salad is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a shared or individual plate at a Myanmar restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17175); adapted for dried and seasoned preparation using USDA drying and seasoning adjustment factors
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutrient entry exists for Burmese dried venison salad in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy venison (raw, NDB 17175) adjusted for drying (approximately 2.5× concentration of protein, fat, and minerals; moisture loss to ~15 g/100g), plus minor additions for fish sauce sodium contribution and sesame/peanut fat contribution. Energy recalculated from adjusted macros using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat salad with low-GI dressing components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese dried venison salad. GI estimated at 35 based on the following reasoning: dried meat has negligible starch content; carbohydrate contribution comes primarily from shallots, garlic, and small amounts of peanuts and sesame — all low-GI foods (GI 10–35 range per University of Sydney GI Database). Mixed meat-dominant salads with low-GI dressing components are consistently classified as low GI. GL per serving = (35 × 8.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 5.74, rounded to 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried meat seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (approximately 1240 mg per standard 200 g serving) represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.7 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; partially offset by lean venison protein and beneficial unsaturated fats from sesame and peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium content from fish sauce seasoning; however, venison is a lean red meat with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, and the inclusion of sesame and peanuts contributes beneficial polyunsaturated fats, making moderate consumption acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sar Tha Jaw Thote (ဆတ်သားခြောက်သုတ်) is a traditional Burmese dried venison salad originating from Myanmar. The dish is prepared by drying and shredding deer (venison) meat, which is then tossed with a seasoned dressing of fish sauce, lime juice, sliced shallots, garlic, dried chilli, toasted sesame seeds, roasted peanuts, and fresh coriander, often finished with crispy fried garlic. The result is a dish with a complex flavour profile: savoury, tangy, mildly spicy, and nutty, with a chewy-to-crispy textural contrast from the dried meat and fried garnishes. Dried venison salad is a culturally significant dish in Myanmar, where venison has historically been a prized game meat, and the salad format (thoke) is a cornerstone of Burmese cuisine. Nutritionally, this dish is protein-dense (approximately 28.5 g protein per 100 g) due to the concentrated dried meat, and provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, and B vitamins characteristic of red meat. The fat content is moderate (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g), with a favourable unsaturated fat profile contributed by sesame and peanuts. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce seasoning (estimated 620 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is low (estimated 35), making this dish appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. In Singapore, this dish is served at Myanmar restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant and represents an authentic regional specialty within the broader Burmese culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Burmese dried venison salad exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy venison data adjusted for drying concentration and seasoning additions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI profile of the dish's carbohydrate components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the primary ingredient (venison) has well-characterised USDA data.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' — the dish is a dried meat salad, not a grain or starch product. The cuisine_tags 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' in the input appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags and have been disregarded. 2. Nutrient values are estimated (medium confidence) — see nutrient_source for full methodology. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA venison source entry and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this preparation. 4. Halal status is marked false as venison sourcing and preparation compliance cannot be confirmed from available data; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the restaurant. 5. GI set to 35 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference data for low-GI meat-dominant salads — see gi_source for full reasoning. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA venison data with drying adjustment factors and are not directly measured for this specific dish.
